attributes.stem:
A 57-year-old male presents with a five year history of numbness and tingling sensation in his right little finger. This is particularly worse during sleep and he is sometimes awoken in the middle of the night due to the discomfort. He also reports occasional pain radiating from the right elbow to the hand. He works as a carpenter and frequently uses a hand saw to cut wood.

The patient has a history of right anterior shoulder dislocation three years ago, which was managed with closed reduction. He has a background of T2DM, for which he is prescribed metformin 1 g twice daily. He consumes one pack of cigarettes daily, and drinks two cans of beer almost every day.

On examination, there is no palpable thickening or nodule in the palm. A photograph of his right hand is shown (image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The photograph shows the late sign of ulnar nerve palsy. The ulnar nerve supplies the small muscles (lumbricals, interossei) of the hand. If this nerve is damaged and these muscles become paralysed, the unopposed action of the extensor muscles will lead to hyperextension across the metacarpophalangeal joints and flexion of the interphalangeal joints. In more severe cases, the claw-like appearance to the hand and fingers would be even more prominent.

Ulnar nerve palsy, also known as cubital tunnel syndrome, involves compression or irritation of the ulnar nerve at the inner side of the elbow, commonly referred to as the "funny bone." This condition can cause numbness, tingling, and pain in the little finger and ulnar side of the ring finger. The patient's occupation as a carpenter involves repetitive elbow flexion while cutting wood, potentially triggering the condition. Additionally, activities that entail sustained elbow flexion, such as lying on one's side or sleeping with the elbow flexed, can worsen the condition. Ulnar nerve subluxation in the cubital tunnel is also common and irritating particularly with repetitive movements.

The diagnosis of ulnar nerve palsy can be confirmed through clinical evaluation, with consideration for dynamic ultrasound or nerve conduction studies. Treatment options include non-operative approaches such as night time splinting and hand therapy, as well as surgical interventions like ulnar nerve neurolysis with elbow release (cubital tunnel release) and, if necessary, ulnar nerve transposition.

Given the chronic onset of the symptoms, the right anterior shoulder dislocation that occurred more recently is unlikely to have contributed to the current episode of ulnar nerve palsy, considering anatomical and time-related factors. If the cause were trauma-related, one would typically expect to observe a claw-like hand deformity shortly after the injury.

Axillary nerve injury is a potential consequence of anterior shoulder dislocation. Patients with this injury may experience weakness or paralysis in the deltoid and teres minor muscles, leading to limited shoulder abduction and external rotation, along with possible sensory deficits in the shoulder region. However, the patient's symptoms do not align with those of axillary nerve injury.

Median nerve palsy can be caused by trauma or compression (as in carpal tunnel syndrome), resulting in loss of sensation over the thenar eminence and the 3 1⁄2 fingers over the radial side, and difficulty in performing fine motor tasks. The "Pope's blessing" hand sign, where patients have difficulty flexing the 3 fingers over the radial side to make a fist, is characteristic.

Volkman's contracture is a permanent forearm muscle deformity resulting from ischaemia. It typically occurs when compartment syndrome is diagnosed late following supracondylar humeral fractures. Contractures develop due to prolonged myonecrosis, resulting in a fibrotic pull on the wrists and fingers. It presents with fingers and wrist flexed, making passive finger extension difficult or impossible.

Dupuytren's contracture is a condition characterised by the progressive fibrosis of the palmar fascia. This fibrous tissue forms nodules and cords, causing the affected fingers, typically the ring and little fingers, to bend inward towards the palm. The exact cause of Dupuytren's contracture is not fully understood, but it is more prevalent in individuals with certain risk factors, including a family history, male gender, diabetes, and alcoholism. However, it is not typically associated with sensory deficits or elbow pain.
learningPoints: Ulnar nerve palsy presents with numbness, tingling, and pain in the 1 1⁄2 fingers over the ulnar side, as well as a claw-like appearance when attempting to extend the fingers.
Although idiopathic cases are fairly common, repetitive or prolonged flexion of the elbow can act as a trigger for ulnar nerve palsy.
references: Silver S, Ledford CC, Vogel KJ, Arnold JJ. Peripheral nerve entrapment and injury in the upper extremity. Am Fam Physician [Internet] . 2021 Mar 1 [cited 2023 Jul 24];103(5):275-85. Available from: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2021/0301/p275.html

Ender S. Neuropathy, ulnar. In: Ettinger AB, Weisbrot DM, editors. Neurologic Differential Diagnosis: A Case-Based Approach. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press; 2014. p. 601–11. Available from: https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/neurologic-differential-diagnosis/neuropathy-ulnar/C120DF568E8A11DD38B69DE808F1893E doi: 10.1017/CBO9781139028899.100
